perturbation.original_text: The Parties and their Representatives (i) will keep the Information strictly confidential and will not (except as required by applicable law, regulation or legal process, and only after compliance with paragraph 2 below), without the prior written consent of the other party, disclose any Information in any manner whatsoever, and (ii) will not use any Information other than in connection with the Transaction; provided, however , that the Parties may reveal the Information to their Representatives (a) who need to know the Information for the purpose of evaluating the Transaction, (b) who are informed by the Parties of the confidential nature of the Information and are provided with a copy of this Agreement, (c) who agree not to disclose any of the Information to any other party and (d) who agree to act in accordance with the terms of this letter agreement to the same extent as if they were parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties and their Representatives (i) will keep the Information strictly confidential and will not (except as required by applicable law, regulation or legal process, and only after compliance with paragraph 2 below), without the prior written consent of the other party, disclose any Information in any manner whatsoever, and (ii) will not use any Information other than in connection with the Transaction; provided, however, that the Parties may reveal the Information to their Representatives (a) who need to know the Information for any purpose, (b) who are informed by the Parties of the confidential nature of the Information and are provided with a copy of this Agreement, (c) who agree not to disclose any of the Information to any other party and (d) who agree to act in accordance with the terms of this letter agreement to the same extent as if they were parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'for the purpose of evaluating the Transaction' to 'for any purpose', the contract potentially allows disclosure of confidential information beyond the specific scope of the transaction, which could violate trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Allowing disclosure 'for any purpose' broadens the scope beyond the legitimate purpose of evaluating the transaction, thus increasing the risk of misappropriation and contradicting the UTSA's intent to protect confidential business information. Disclosure must be tied to the Transaction, not general use.  The change is from using the information for 'evaluating the transaction' to 'any purpose'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original contract restricts information use to 'evaluating the Transaction.' The modified text broadens this to 'any purpose.' The Law Explanation states that this change increases the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets, contradicting the intent of the UTSA (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) to protect confidential business information, because disclosure must be tied to the transaction, and not general use. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Economic Espionage Act which shows the importance of maintaining trade secret protections.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Parties or any of their Representatives are requested pursuant to, or required by, applicable law, regulation, rules of any national stock exchange, or legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Parties must notify each other promptly so that they may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy or, in the sole discretion of the party that initially furnished such Information, waive compliance with the terms of this letter agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Parties or any of their Representatives are requested pursuant to, or required by, applicable law, regulation, rules of any national stock exchange, or legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Parties may choose to notify each other so that they may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy or, in the sole discretion of the party that initially furnished such Information, waive compliance with the terms of this letter agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must notify' to 'may choose to notify' weakens the obligation to notify the other party about a legal request for information. This could prevent a party from seeking a protective order, potentially violating discovery rules in litigation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 26 governs the scope of discovery in federal civil cases.  While it doesn't explicitly mandate notification in all cases, it implies a duty to act in good faith and cooperate in discovery. By making notification optional, the modified text allows a party to unilaterally decide whether to disclose information requested in discovery, potentially obstructing the other party's ability to obtain necessary information and thus violating the spirit of Rule 26.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Read the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (PDF) Related Downloads Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 431.61 KB Return to top Your browser does not support Javascript. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'must notify' to 'may choose to notify' significantly weakens the obligation to inform the other party of a legal request for information. This directly affects the other party's ability to seek a protective order or other remedies. The Law Explanation states this obstructs the other party's ability to obtain necessary information and violates the spirit of Rule 26. Scraped Law Snippet 1 describes the 'Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery'. While it focuses on initial disclosures, the underlying principle of cooperation and disclosure in discovery is undermined by making notification optional, thus contradicting the intended spirit and procedural fairness inherent in the rules. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is 'to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding.' Making notification optional could lead to delays and increased costs as parties might need to independently discover the information, undermining this purpose.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Accordingly, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the breaching party agrees that the non-breaching party may be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Accordingly, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the breaching party acknowledges that the non-breaching party may request equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance. The availability of such relief is subject to judicial discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'agrees that the non-breaching party may be entitled to' to 'acknowledges that the non-breaching party may request' and adding 'The availability of such relief is subject to judicial discretion', the contract introduces uncertainty about the availability of equitable relief. This contradicts the principle that parties can contractually agree on remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Specific Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: While courts retain discretion in granting specific performance, parties can contractually agree that money damages are inadequate, strengthening the argument for specific performance.  The modified text weakens this contractual agreement by emphasizing judicial discretion, undermining the parties' ability to determine remedies in advance and casting doubt on the availability of specific performance. The addition that the availability of such relief is subject to judicial discretion makes it seem that the parties are not in agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original text states the breaching party 'agrees' the non-breaching party is 'entitled' to equitable relief, suggesting a mutual agreement on the availability of such relief as a remedy. The changed text weakens this by stating the non-breaching party may 'request' equitable relief, and explicitly subjects the availability of such relief to 'judicial discretion'. This change contradicts the principle that parties can contractually agree on remedies, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that specific performance is a contractual remedy where the court orders a party to fulfill their obligations 'as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract'. The modified text introduces uncertainty, undermining the enforceability of the originally agreed-upon remedy, making the contract deviate from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: COMPANY agrees that, for a period of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, unless COMPANY shall have been specifically invited in writing by COMSYS, neither COMPANY nor its Representatives as advisor to COMPANY as principal will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in or in any way assist any other person to effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in, (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of COMSYS or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving COMSYS or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to COMSYS or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the other Party
perturbation.changed_text: COMPANY agrees that, for a period of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, unless COMPANY shall have been specifically invited orally by COMSYS, neither COMPANY nor its Representatives as advisor to COMPANY as principal will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in or in any way assist any other person to effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in, (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of COMSYS or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving COMSYS or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to COMSYS or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the other Party
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'specifically invited in writing by COMSYS' to 'specifically invited orally by COMSYS' creates an ambiguity that affects the enforceability of the clause. An oral invitation is harder to prove and thus undermines the agreement and increases chances of unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds in New York (where the contract is governed) requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. While this specific agreement might not always fall under the Statute of Frauds, the change to allowing oral invitations creates a situation where proving an exception to the standstill provision becomes significantly more difficult. This could potentially make the entire agreement unenforceable, as courts might be hesitant to enforce a poorly defined standstill provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'specifically invited in writing' to 'specifically invited orally' introduces ambiguity and difficulty in proving an exception to the standstill provision. This directly undermines the enforceability of the agreement, which is a key concern addressed by the Statute of Frauds. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith...1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof'. While the standstill agreement itself might not automatically fall under the Statute of Frauds, making an exception to it provable only by oral agreement significantly weakens the overall contract and makes it less likely to be enforced. Therefore, a contradiction exists.
